statement: Audio transcription accuracy is governed by three factors — audio quality, speaker separation (diarization), and domain vocabulary — and optimizing only one while ignoring the others produces transcripts that are technically high-WER (word error rate) but practically useless. Whisper (OpenAI) achieves state-of-the-art WER on clean audio but degrades significantly with crosstalk, background noise, and domain-specific jargon (medical, legal, technical). The WHY transcription is the highest-leverage step in the meeting intelligence pipeline: every downstream capability — summarization, action item extraction, decision logging — is bottlenecked by transcript quality. A 10% WER means 1 in 10 words is wrong; in a 60-minute meeting with ~9,000 words, that is 900 incorrect words, enough to corrupt action items and misattribute statements. The practical implication: invest in pre-processing (noise cancellation, automatic gain control) before transcription, and choose a provider with strong diarization (AssemblyAI, Deepgram) for multi-speaker meetings rather than defaulting to the lowest-cost option.

Meeting summarization requires a different strategy depending on meeting type: decision-making meetings need a structured output (decisions made, owners, deadlines), brainstorming meetings need a thematic clustering of ideas, status meetings need a per-topic status table, and one-on-ones need a private summary focused on commitments and relationship context. Applying a single generic summarization prompt to all meeting types produces outputs that are technically accurate but not actionable — a decision meeting summary that reads as a narrative paragraph is harder to act on than a bulleted decision log. The WHY meeting type matters for prompt design: the same conversation can produce very different summaries depending on the lens (what was decided vs. what was discussed vs. what was felt). AI agents should classify meeting type at the start of processing (from calendar invite title/description, agenda text, or a brief classification LLM call) and route to a type-specific summarization prompt. This doubles the practical utility of the output without significantly increasing cost.

Action item extraction from meeting transcripts fails most often on implicit commitments — statements like 'I'll take care of that' or 'let's make sure that gets done' — rather than explicit ones like 'John will deliver the spec by Friday.' The WHY implicit commitments are the harder problem: they require discourse understanding (tracking who 'I' refers to at this point in the conversation, what 'that' refers to given the prior topic) rather than simple pattern matching. Models that extract action items via keyword matching ('will', 'follow up', 'action') achieve roughly 60–70% recall and miss a significant fraction of real commitments. LLM-based extraction with speaker-attributed transcripts and a carefully designed extraction prompt achieves 85–92% recall on well-diarized transcripts. The most reliable prompt strategy: ask the LLM to identify action items in two passes — first extract explicit commitments, then extract implicit agreements and soft commitments, and deduplicate. Include speaker labels in the prompt context so the model can correctly attribute ownership.

OpenAI Whisper API accepts audio files up to 25MB in mp3, mp4, mpeg, mpga, m4a, wav, or webm format via POST /v1/audio/transcriptions. For meetings longer than approximately 30 minutes, the audio file will exceed the 25MB limit and must be chunked. The chunking strategy: (1) Split at silence boundaries (not fixed time intervals) using pydub or ffmpeg silence detection to avoid cutting words mid-utterance, (2) Maintain a 2-second overlap between chunks to recover words cut at boundaries, (3) Merge chunk transcripts with overlap deduplication. For production pipelines, run Whisper locally (whisper.cpp or faster-whisper) to avoid per-minute API costs on high volumes — local Whisper large-v3 runs at ~10–15x real-time on A100 GPU. AssemblyAI's async API (POST /v2/transcript) handles files up to 5GB natively and includes automatic diarization, making it the better choice for long recordings without chunking complexity. Always request word-level timestamps from either API to enable downstream speaker-segment alignment.

AssemblyAI transcription with diarization uses a two-step async workflow: (1) Upload audio to POST https://api.assemblyai.com/v2/upload (returns upload_url), (2) Submit transcription job to POST /v2/transcript with body {audio_url: upload_url, speaker_labels: true, speakers_expected: N}. Poll GET /v2/transcript/{id} until status is 'completed' or 'error' — use exponential backoff starting at 5 seconds. The response includes utterances array with speaker label, text, start/end timestamps per word. A critical configuration decision: speakers_expected defaults to auto-detection but performs better when the number of speakers is known — for scheduled meetings, pass the attendee count from the calendar event. AssemblyAI also offers real-time streaming transcription via WebSocket (wss://api.assemblyai.com/v2/realtime/ws) for live meeting scenarios; the streaming endpoint sends partial_transcript and final_transcript events as the audio is processed. Pricing is per audio minute — batch the smallest necessary audio segment to minimize cost.

Google Calendar API integration for meeting intelligence pipelines uses OAuth 2.0 with the https://www.googleapis.com/auth/calendar.readonly scope for read access. The primary workflow: (1) Subscribe to the Calendar push notification channel via POST https://www.googleapis.com/calendar/v3/calendars/{calendarId}/events/watch with a webhook URL and channelId — this replaces polling and delivers event change notifications within seconds, (2) On receipt of a change notification, fetch the updated event via GET /v3/calendars/{calendarId}/events/{eventId} to get attendees, conference data (Google Meet link), and description/agenda, (3) On event end (compare current time vs. event.end.dateTime), trigger the meeting processing pipeline. A practical footgun: the push notification channel expires after a maximum of 7 days for Google Calendar and must be renewed before expiry via another watch request — implement an automated renewal job that re-subscribes every 6 days. For Google Meet recordings, use the Meet API (currently in limited preview) or integrate with the third-party recording bot approach (Recall.ai, Fireflies.ai).

Microsoft Outlook/Teams calendar integration uses Microsoft Graph API with OAuth 2.0 (client credentials or delegated flow). The subscription-based event notification endpoint: POST https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/subscriptions with resource='/me/events', changeType='created,updated,deleted', notificationUrl pointing to your webhook. Graph subscriptions for calendar expire after 3 days maximum and must be renewed via PATCH /subscriptions/{id}. For Teams meeting recordings: Teams stores recordings in SharePoint/OneDrive after the meeting ends — subscribe to the OneDrive change notification for the specific folder where recordings are saved, or use the Graph callRecords API (https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/communications/callRecords) which provides call metadata including participant list, start/end time, and modalities. Teams does not yet provide a native API for real-time transcription delivery to external services; the practical approach is to enable Teams auto-transcription and retrieve the VTT transcript file from SharePoint via Graph after the meeting ends.

Jira task creation from meeting action items uses the Jira REST API v3. The endpoint for issue creation is POST https://{domain}.atlassian.net/rest/api/3/issue with Basic Auth (email + API token) or OAuth 2.0 (3LO). The request body requires fields.project.key (Jira project key), fields.issuetype.name ('Task' or 'Story'), and fields.summary. For action items extracted from meetings, also populate: fields.description (the full context sentence from the transcript), fields.assignee.accountId (requires looking up the Jira accountId from the attendee's email via GET /rest/api/3/user/search?query={email}), fields.duedate (ISO 8601 date from the action item deadline if extracted), and a custom field linking back to the source meeting (requires creating a custom field of type URL in Jira first). Always set fields.priority based on urgency language in the action item ('urgent', 'ASAP', 'critical' → High priority). The Jira Cloud rate limit is 10 requests/second per app — batch issue creation via the bulk create endpoint POST /rest/api/3/issue/bulk for meetings with many action items.

Linear task creation from meeting action items uses the Linear GraphQL API (https://api.linear.app/graphql) with API key or OAuth 2.0. The mutation for creating an issue is IssueCreate with input fields: title, description (markdown), teamId, assigneeId, dueDate (ISO 8601), priority (0=none, 1=urgent, 2=high, 3=medium, 4=low), and stateId. To resolve assigneeId from an email address, use the users query filtered by email. A useful pattern for meeting integration: create a dedicated 'Meeting Actions' team or label in Linear and route all meeting-generated tasks there for triage, rather than scattering them across project-specific teams immediately — this gives the team a weekly ritual of reviewing and promoting tasks to the appropriate project. Linear's webhooks deliver real-time updates on issue state changes, enabling follow-up tracking: subscribe to IssueUpdate events and update the meeting record when tasks are completed. Linear enforces a 1,500 request/hour rate limit for API integrations.

Todoist task creation from meeting action items uses the Todoist REST API v2 (https://api.todoist.com/rest/v2). Authentication is via Bearer token (personal or OAuth). Create a task via POST /tasks with content (task title), description (context), due_date (YYYY-MM-DD), priority (1=normal, 2=high, 3=very high, 4=urgent), project_id, and assignee_id (for shared projects). For meeting pipelines, maintain a dedicated Todoist project per meeting series (e.g., 'Weekly Eng Standup Actions') and create tasks under it. The Todoist sync API (https://api.todoist.com/sync/v9/sync) is more efficient for bulk creation: it accepts a commands array where each command is an action type ('item_add') with a temp_id and args object — a single HTTP request can create 10+ tasks atomically. Todoist webhooks fire on task.completed events, enabling the meeting record to be updated when all action items are closed. Important: Todoist's assignee feature requires a Todoist Business account — individual plan users can only create tasks in their own inbox.

Meeting agenda generation before a recurring meeting should be automated by pulling context from multiple sources: (1) Open action items from the previous meeting (query task manager API filtered by meeting label and status=open), (2) Relevant Jira/Linear tickets assigned to attendees that have been updated since the last meeting, (3) Key metric changes from the business intelligence system (revenue, churn, DAU delta), (4) Any explicit agenda items submitted by attendees via a shared document or form. Pass all collected context to an LLM with the prompt: 'Generate a 45-minute meeting agenda for <meeting_title> with attendees <list>. Prioritize unresolved action items from last week. Include time allocations for each item. Flag any item that requires a decision vs. just an update.' Send the generated agenda to attendees 24 hours before the meeting via calendar event update (Calendar API) or email. The WHY automating agenda generation matters: meetings without agendas run 35% longer on average and produce fewer decisions per minute according to research on meeting effectiveness.

Decision logging from meeting transcripts requires a structured extraction schema separate from action items because decisions have different attributes: a decision has a context (what was being debated), the decision itself (what was resolved), the rationale (why this option was chosen), the decision maker or group, and the date. The LLM extraction prompt should return a JSON array of decision objects matching this schema. Key implementation detail: store decisions in a dedicated table (not mixed with tasks) with a foreign key to the meeting record and a taggable taxonomy (product, engineering, sales, etc.) so decisions can be searched historically — 'what did we decide about pricing six months ago?' is a high-value query that organizations currently cannot answer without searching through meeting notes manually. Index decisions by keyword and use semantic search (vector embeddings) for the historical query use case. Surface the decision log in a read-only Notion database or Confluence space updated automatically after each relevant meeting.

Follow-up tracking for meeting action items requires closing the loop between task creation and meeting records. The pattern: (1) When tasks are created from a meeting, store (task_id, task_system, meeting_id, due_date, owner_email) in a meeting_actions table, (2) Run a daily job that queries each task system API for status updates on open tasks, (3) Compute completion metrics per meeting: tasks_total, tasks_completed, tasks_overdue, (4) If any tasks are overdue by more than 3 days, send an automated nudge to the task owner (not the meeting organizer) via Slack or email citing the specific task and original due date. The nudge should include the original meeting context: 'In the Tuesday standup, you committed to X by Y date — still open.' Context-rich nudges have significantly higher completion rates than generic 'you have overdue tasks' notifications. Store completion rate per meeting series over time to identify chronic follow-through problems in specific teams or recurring meetings.

FAILURE MODE: Generating a meeting summary from a transcript without preserving speaker attribution produces summaries that misrepresent who said what — a critical problem in decision meetings where accountability matters. When an LLM summarizes 'the team agreed to delay the launch' but the transcript shows only one stakeholder pushed for the delay while others raised objections, the summary obscures a meaningful disagreement that the team should have on record. THE FIX: require the transcript to include speaker labels (diarization) and instruct the LLM to attribute statements to specific speakers: 'Sarah (VP Eng): recommended delaying launch due to test coverage concerns. John (CEO): agreed contingent on resolving critical bugs by Friday.' For one-on-ones and sensitive meetings, give attendees the option to opt out of speaker-attributed summaries and receive an un-attributed version. Never use speaker-attributed summaries for performance evaluation without explicit disclosure to participants — this creates legal and trust risks.

FAILURE MODE: Running Whisper on raw Zoom or Teams audio without pre-processing causes transcription quality degradation that is invisible until you read the output. Common issues: background echo from participants without proper headsets produces double-transcribed words; multiple people talking simultaneously creates word salad at those segments; low-volume remote participants get under-transcribed. THE FIX: (1) Apply noise reduction before transcription using a library like noisereduce (Python) or ffmpeg's anlmdn filter, (2) Normalize audio gain to -14 LUFS using ffmpeg's loudnorm filter — this improves recognition of quiet speakers dramatically, (3) For recordings with severe crosstalk, separate speaker tracks where possible (Zoom allows separate audio tracks per participant in cloud recordings) and transcribe tracks independently before merging. Additionally, Whisper struggles with non-English accents on technical vocabulary — if your team is multilingual, evaluate Whisper large-v3 vs. AssemblyAI Best model vs. Deepgram Nova-2 on a sample of your actual audio before committing to a provider.

Async meeting alternatives — recorded video briefs, Loom-style async updates, shared documents with threaded comments — reduce meeting load but generate their own transcript and action item processing needs. For Loom or similar recorded async updates: (1) Use Loom's API (https://dev.loom.com/docs) webhook for video.transcription.completed events to retrieve the auto-generated transcript, (2) Process the transcript with the same meeting intelligence pipeline (summarization + action item extraction), (3) Post the summary as a threaded comment on the Loom video so viewers get the TL;DR without watching the full recording. For Notion or Confluence async documents with comment threads: export comments via the respective APIs and pass to an LLM to extract any embedded commitments or decisions from the discussion. The key insight: async communication generates as much implicit commitment and decision data as synchronous meetings, but organizations rarely apply the same processing to it — capturing async decisions is a competitive advantage for distributed teams.

Handling long meeting transcripts that exceed LLM context windows requires a hierarchical summarization strategy, not naive truncation. Naive truncation (taking only the first N tokens) discards the end of the meeting which often contains the most important content: decisions, next steps, and commitments typically accumulate toward the end of a meeting as the discussion converges. The hierarchical approach: (1) Split the transcript into segments of 10–15 minutes each (respecting speaker turn boundaries), (2) Summarize each segment independently with a segment-specific prompt, (3) Concatenate segment summaries and generate the final meeting summary from segment summaries, (4) Run action item extraction on the full transcript with a separate pass that uses only the sentence windows around commitment-indicating language patterns, reducing token usage by 60–70%. For 3-hour meetings, a three-level hierarchy (segment → section → meeting) may be necessary. Store segment summaries alongside the final summary for auditability — reviewers who dispute the final summary can drill down to the segment level.

Meeting recording consent and privacy compliance is a legal requirement that varies significantly by jurisdiction: one-party consent states (US federal + most US states) allow recording with only one participant's knowledge; two-party/all-party consent states (California, Florida, Illinois, and others) require all participants to consent. For cross-border meetings (US + EU), GDPR Article 6 requires a lawful basis for processing voice data, and Article 13 requires informing participants of the data processing purpose and retention period. The safe implementation: (1) Play or display a consent disclosure at meeting start ('This meeting will be recorded and transcribed for notes. Press *9 to opt out.'), (2) Store consent acknowledgment per participant per meeting, (3) Apply data retention limits — delete raw audio after 30 days, anonymized transcripts after 1 year unless a participant requests earlier deletion (GDPR right to erasure), (4) Never process meeting audio from jurisdictions where you do not have verified consent. Embed consent status as metadata on the meeting record before triggering the transcription pipeline.

FAILURE MODE: Creating Jira or Linear tasks directly from raw LLM action item extraction output without a normalization step produces tasks with vague titles like 'Follow up on the thing from the discussion' or impossibly broad scope like 'Fix the entire onboarding flow.' These tasks sit in backlogs indefinitely and erode team trust in the meeting intelligence system. THE FIX: apply a quality gate prompt after extraction: 'Review each action item and rewrite it to: (1) start with an action verb, (2) name a specific owner, (3) include a measurable deliverable, (4) specify a deadline. If any element is missing and cannot be inferred from context, flag the item as NEEDS_CLARIFICATION instead of creating it.' Items flagged as NEEDS_CLARIFICATION are queued for a 5-minute human review session (not created automatically). Track the ratio of auto-created to NEEDS_CLARIFICATION items over time — a ratio above 30% indicates the meeting itself lacks sufficient structure and the real fix is improving meeting facilitation, not the AI extraction system.

Real-time meeting transcription with live action item detection enables in-meeting nudges: the facilitator can see a sidebar showing detected commitments as they are made, reducing post-meeting processing load and catching ambiguous commitments while the speaker is still on the call to clarify. Implementation using AssemblyAI real-time WebSocket: (1) Capture microphone audio via browser MediaRecorder API (16kHz, PCM16 or linear16), (2) Stream audio chunks to the AssemblyAI WebSocket endpoint which returns partial and final transcript segments, (3) Buffer completed sentences and run a lightweight action item detection prompt on each 5-sentence window (not the full transcript to stay within latency budget), (4) Display detected items in a floating panel visible to the facilitator only. Target end-to-end latency from speech to detection display of under 3 seconds — AssemblyAI real-time typically adds 1–1.5 seconds of transcription latency; the detection LLM call should be limited to a small model (GPT-4o-mini or Claude Haiku) to keep total latency under budget.

Speaker identification beyond diarization (assigning Speaker 1/Speaker 2 labels) requires mapping diarized speaker segments to actual names using one of three techniques: (1) Voice enrollment: pre-record 30+ seconds of each known participant and build speaker embeddings using a model like pyannote.audio or Azure Speaker Recognition — match live diarized segments to enrolled embeddings, (2) Calendar-based attribution: for meetings with known attendees, use the calendar invite attendee list as a roster and prompt the LLM to identify speakers from contextual clues (name mentions, role references) in the transcript, (3) Explicit verbal identification: detect speaker introduction patterns ('Hi, I'm Sarah...') in the first 2 minutes of the meeting and use them to anchor the diarized Speaker labels to real names. Technique (2) is the most practical for meetings with clear attendee lists; technique (1) is more accurate but requires enrollment infrastructure. Never display identified speaker labels to third parties without explicit consent from each speaker.

FAILURE MODE: Using calendar event end time as the trigger for post-meeting processing assumes meetings always end on schedule, which is false — back-to-back meetings cause late starts, discussions run over, and the meeting may end 10 minutes early or 30 minutes late. Triggering at the scheduled end time means processing starts before the meeting ends (incomplete transcript) or misses processing the overrun portion. THE FIX: trigger post-meeting processing based on actual audio/recording end signal, not calendar end time. For Zoom, use the recording.completed webhook. For Google Meet, use the Meet API event when available, or poll the Drive folder for new recording files. For phone calls via dialers, use the call.ended webhook. As a fallback when real end-signal is unavailable, delay processing by 30 minutes after the scheduled end time — this trades immediacy for completeness and is the pragmatic default. Never hardcode a fixed offset; instead, make it a configuration parameter so it can be tuned per meeting type.

Meeting intelligence output delivery should be tailored to the consumer: the meeting organizer needs the full structured output (summary + decisions + all action items), participants need only their own action items, stakeholders who did not attend need the executive summary and decisions only. Implement a distribution matrix: (1) On processing completion, query the meeting record for attendee roles and their notification preferences, (2) Render three templates from the same structured data — full output, personal action items, and executive brief, (3) Deliver via the recipient's preferred channel: Slack DM, email, or a Notion page update. For Slack delivery: use the conversations.open API to get the DM channel ID for each recipient by email, then chat.postMessage with a Block Kit formatted message that includes clickable task links. For email delivery: use a transactional email API (SendGrid, Postmark) with a pre-designed meeting summary HTML template. Store delivery receipts per recipient to enable re-send on failure.

FAILURE MODE: Building meeting intelligence as a single synchronous API call (receive audio → transcribe → summarize → create tasks → notify, all in one request) creates a brittle system that fails completely on any step failure and times out on long meetings. A 2-hour meeting takes 8–15 minutes to process end-to-end; no HTTP timeout should be set that long, and no user should wait synchronously. THE FIX: decompose into a durable task queue pipeline: (1) Receive audio upload → enqueue 'transcribe' job, return job_id immediately with 202 Accepted, (2) Transcription worker completes → enqueues 'analyze' job with transcript_id, (3) Analysis worker completes → enqueues 'deliver' jobs per recipient, (4) Each delivery job fires independently with its own retry policy. Store the full pipeline state per meeting in a jobs table with status per step. This architecture means any step can be retried independently on failure without re-running the expensive transcription step. Expose a status endpoint (GET /meetings/{id}/status) so the client can poll or subscribe to SSE updates.

Recurring meeting intelligence requires longitudinal context to be useful: a weekly team standup summary is more valuable if it compares this week's blockers to last week's blockers and tracks which items from last week were resolved. Implement a meeting series context window: for each recurring meeting (identified by calendar series_id or recurring meeting pattern), maintain a rolling context document containing the last 4 meeting summaries, open action items, and persistent decisions. Prepend this context to the summarization prompt: 'Given the following history of this weekly standup over the past 4 weeks, summarize this week's meeting with explicit callouts of: (a) new items not present last week, (b) recurring blockers mentioned in 3+ consecutive meetings, (c) action items from previous meetings that are now resolved.' This longitudinal summarization surface patterns that per-meeting summaries miss — a blocker mentioned weekly for 6 weeks is a system problem, not a one-off issue.
